case_name: Woods v Doncaster And Bassetlaw Teaching Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust [2024] EWHC 1432 (KB) (11 June 2024)

summary.brief: Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: The claimant was Julie Woods' first child. Mrs Woods attended her general practitioner on 19 February 1998 when the pregnancy was confirmed.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On 5 June Mrs Woods attended the hospital feeling unwell since the previous day and complained of feeling nauseous, lightheaded and with intermittent cramping pain in her lower abdomen.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The claimant brings this action for damages for personal injury arising from the alleged negligent delay in her delivery by the medical and nursing staff of the Doncaster Royal Infirmary.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mrs Woods was transferred to the operating theatre at 05.02. At 05.10 the fetal heart was auscultated and found to be 136 bpm.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The claimant was delivered at 05.20 with the cord wrapped once around the neck.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The claimant's Apgar scores included the value of 3 at 1 minute.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: It is not contentious that the claimant's condition in the first 24 hours of life was consistent with hypoxic ischaemic encephalopathy.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mr Hare said that it was unacceptable for Dr Samy to have concluded that the trace was reassuring.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert testimony

evidence_index.text: Mr Hare said that at birth, the claimant had intrauterine growth retardation due to placental insufficiency.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert testimony

evidence_index.text: Mr Tuffnell said that a tachycardia with normal variability was in his experience almost always caused by the baby moving.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert testimony

evidence_index.text: Mrs Woods' statement (dated August 2009) records that on 28 September 1998 she had felt a gush of fluid from her vagina.
evidence_index.speaker: Mrs Woods

evidence_index.text: Mrs Woods said that, after 28 September, she remained concerned about the pregnancy and in particular the lack of fetal movement.
evidence_index.speaker: Mrs Woods

evidence_index.text: Judgment should be entered for the claimant.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: I am satisfied that the decision by Dr Samy to instigate further CTG monitoring after a break of one hour was appropriate and reasonable management.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: The second trace did not provide sufficient reassurance to justify allowing Mrs Woods to go home with routine antenatal follow up.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.chief_complaint: History of leaking per vaginum and reduced fetal movements
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Mrs Woods, at 39 weeks' gestation, self-referred to the hospital on 28 September 1998, reporting a history of leaking fluid since midday and contractions that morning.

focused_exam.obstetric: Fetal heart rate noted to be 160-175 bpm on CTG

reveal.result_summary: First trace showed tachycardia and two decelerations; second trace was non-reassuring
reveal.decision_impact: Should have prompted further investigation and possible early delivery

requestables.name: Obstetric Consultation
requestables.description: Consultation with Dr Samy regarding CTG interpretation

reveal.result_summary: Dr Samy concluded the trace was reassuring and discharged Mrs Woods
reveal.clinical_significance: Decision to discharge was contested as unreasonable
reveal.decision_impact: Led to delayed delivery and potential harm

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Mrs Woods presents to hospital with concerns about fetal movements and leaking fluid

timeline_phases.name: CTG Monitoring and Decision
timeline_phases.description: CTG monitoring and decision to discharge

decision_points.clinical_context: Mrs Woods presented with potential fetal distress
decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step after reviewing the CTG trace?

options.description: Admit for further monitoring and possible early delivery
options.clinical_reasoning: Non-reassuring CTG should prompt further investigation

expected_action_court.description: Further monitoring and assessment should have been conducted
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence indicated need for further investigation

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Based on evidence E020-E021, the trace was non-reassuring
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Further monitoring and possible early delivery

patient_outcome.description: The claimant was delivered with intrauterine growth retardation and signs of fetal distress.
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Chronic health issues related to birth complications

key_findings: Failure to induce labor despite signs of fetal distress
Causation established - earlier intervention would have prevented complications

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Induction would have prevented fetal distress

factual_timeline.event: Mrs. Woods attended the hospital feeling unwell
